content:
Cloud adoption at enterprise scale fails when it lacks a foundation. Without a structured landing zone, subscriptions proliferate as unmanaged silos, security policies are applied inconsistently, and networking becomes a chaotic web of peerings and overlapping IP ranges. “Just create a subscription and start building” is a strategy that leads to a $50,000 month-end surprise and a security audit you won’t pass.

An Azure Landing Zone (ALZ) is the architectural anchor for your cloud estate. It provides the shared services — networking, identity, governance, and logging — that every application needs but no single team should manage alone. In 2026, the standard for building these foundations has shifted from monolithic modules to the Azure Verified Modules (AVM) initiative, emphasizing “Metadata-First” architectures and secret-less CI/CD.

By the end of this guide, you will be able to:

  • Design a management group hierarchy that balances global governance with team autonomy
  • Deploy a hub-and-spoke network with centralized egress and private name resolution
  • Implement a “Zero Standing Access” identity model using PIM and OIDC
  • Automate subscription onboarding through a PR-driven vending pipeline
  • Maintain your foundation using automated drift detection and policy remediation

This is the Pillar Post for the Azure Platform Engineering series. Across the next 10 articles, we will build every layer of this architecture using production-grade Bicep and Terraform.

1. The Anatomy of an Enterprise Landing Zone

A landing zone is not a single resource; it is a multi-layered ecosystem designed according to the Cloud Adoption Framework (CAF). Microsoft divides this ecosystem into eight distinct design areas, ranging from billing and identity to networking and operations.

Platform vs. Application Landing Zones

The most critical architectural distinction is between the Platform and the Application.

  • Platform Landing Zones: These are the shared services subscriptions (Connectivity, Identity, Management). They provide the hub VNet, the Entra ID tenant, and the central Log Analytics Workspace.
  • Application Landing Zones: These are the workload subscriptions where your apps actually run. They are “consumers” of the platform, inheriting its security policies and peering back to the hub.

ALZ Management Group Hierarchy

The hierarchy is the “Gavel” of your landing zone. It determines how policies and permissions cascade down to resources.

2. Design Area 1: Network Isolation (Hub-and-Spoke)

Networking is the foundation of isolation. In a landing zone, we use a Hub-and-Spoke topology to centralize shared network services and enforce a single egress point to the internet.

Hub-and-Spoke Core Architecture

In 2026, the Azure DNS Private Resolver is the standard for hybrid name resolution, replacing the need for complex, manual VM-based DNS forwarders. When combined with Azure Firewall Premium, you gain deep packet inspection and TLS termination at the network boundary.

3. Design Area 2: Identity & Governance (Zero Trust)

API keys and standing “Owner” permissions are the primary risks in the modern cloud. Our blueprint moves to a “Keyless and Standing-Access-Free” model.

Managed Identity and OIDC

For CI/CD, we use Workload Identity Federation (OIDC). GitHub Actions and Azure DevOps runners authenticate to Azure using short-lived tokens, eliminating the need to store secrets in your repository settings.

Privileged Identity Management (PIM)

For human administrators, we enforce PIM-only access. No user holds a permanent “Owner” or “Contributor” role. Instead, they are eligible for the role and must activate it for a 4-8 hour window with a business justification and MFA.

# Example: Assigning a Management Group Contributor role to a pipeline SPN at root.
# Note: This requires Global Administrator privileges to execute.
az role assignment create \
  --role "Management Group Contributor" \
  --scope "/" \
  --assignee "<service-principal-object-id>"

4. Building with Azure Verified Modules (AVM)

In 2026, Microsoft retired two predecessor approaches: the monolithic terraform-azurerm-caf-enterprise-scale module and the classic ALZ-Bicep repository. They have been unified into Azure Verified Modules (AVM).

AVM modules are atomic, high-quality, and follows a strict “Contract” for parameters and outputs. This allows you to mix-and-match modules from both Terraform and Bicep while maintaining a consistent security posture.

Terraform AVM Example

module "spoke_vnet" {
  source  = "Azure/avm-res-network-virtualnetwork/azurerm"
  version = "~> 0.7"

  name                = "vnet-prod-app-001"
  resource_group_name = "rg-prod-app-001"
  location            = "eastus"
  address_space       = ["10.1.0.0/16"]

  # AVM includes standard telemetry and diagnostic support
  diagnostic_settings = {
    to_central_law = {
      workspace_resource_id = var.log_analytics_workspace_id # Full Resource ID
    }
  }
}

5. The Deployment Lifecycle (CI/CD)

Deploying a landing zone from a laptop is a single point of failure. A production-grade foundation requires a GitOps workflow.

The Deployment Lifecycle (AVM + CI/CD)

6. Hands-On: Scaffolding Your Landing Zone

To get started, we recommend the ALZ Accelerator. This tool generates the repository structure, OIDC identities, and initial GitHub Actions workflows for your specific environment.

# Install the ALZ Accelerator PowerShell module
Install-Module -Name ALZ -Force -Scope CurrentUser

# Scaffolding a new environment for GitHub Actions
New-ALZEnvironment -Path "C:\Source\MyALZ" -DeploymentStrategy "GitHubActions"

Key Takeaways

  1. Defaults are Risks: Azure’s default public endpoints and standing permissions must be explicitly hardened for enterprise use.
  2. Hierarchy is the Foundation: A well-designed Management Group tree is the only way to scale policy and cost controls.
  3. Standardize on AVM: Stop building custom modules for standard Azure resources. Use Microsoft-verified patterns to reduce your maintenance debt.
  4. Shift-Left with PRs: Every change to the platform must be previewed via terraform plan or bicep what-if before a human signs off.

The transition to an enterprise landing zone is an ongoing process. As you follow the Azure Platform Engineering series, we will deep-dive into each layer — from the initial MG hierarchy to Day-2 drift detection.

Sources

  • Microsoft CAF: What is an Azure Landing Zone?
  • Azure Landing Zones (ALZ) Accelerator
  • Azure Verified Modules (AVM)
  • CAF Enterprise-Scale FAQ
description: Build a production-grade Azure Landing Zone from scratch. Covers all 8 CAF design areas, Terraform AVM, Bicep Deployment Stacks, and secret-less CI/CD.
title: Azure Platform Engineering: The Complete Guide to Building an Enterprise Landing Zone

content:
The hierarchy is the first thing you build in a landing zone, and it is the hardest thing to change later. Management Groups (MGs) provide a scope above the subscription level, allowing you to apply Azure Policies and RBAC roles that cascade down to every child resource. A well-designed tree enables you to govern 1,000 subscriptions with the same effort as one. A poorly designed tree—typically one that mirrors a fluctuating organizational chart—leads to constant refactoring and security gaps.

In an enterprise environment, we design for Functional Longevity. Your hierarchy should represent the “Job to be Done” (Connectivity, Production Workloads, Sandboxes) rather than which department is currently paying the bill. This guide teaches you how to deploy the standard Cloud Adoption Framework (CAF) hierarchy using Azure Verified Modules (AVM) and establish the governance scaffold for your entire Azure estate.

1. The Enterprise-Scale Hierarchy

The standard blueprint starts with a single “Intermediate” Management Group below the Tenant Root. This group represents your organization and serves as the boundary for all shared enterprise policies.

Enterprise-Scale Management Group Hierarchy

1.1: Functional Segments

  • Platform: Home to the shared service subscriptions. No application code runs here.
  • Landing Zones: The production and non-production environments for applications.
    • Corp: Workloads requiring internal connectivity (VPN/ER).
    • Online: Public-facing workloads with direct internet ingress.
  • Sandbox: A “Free Fire” zone for experimentation. Policies here are loose (Audit-only) and resources are often auto-deleted after 30 days.
  • Decommissioned: The “Parking Lot” for subscriptions slated for deletion. A single Deny All policy here ensures no new costs are incurred while waiting for the purge cycle.

2. Policy and RBAC Cascading

The value of the hierarchy is the automated inheritance of controls.

Policy and RBAC Cascading

3. Deploying with Terraform AVM

The avm-ptn-alz module is the 2026 standard for deploying the CAF hierarchy. It replaces the legacy caf-enterprise-scale module with a flatter, more maintainable structure.

# Deploy the MG Hierarchy
module "alz" {
  source  = "Azure/avm-ptn-alz/azurerm"
  version = "~> 0.10"

  management_group_name = "contoso"
  
  # Standard CAF archetypes are built-in
  enable_telemetry = false
}

# Placing a subscription into the hierarchy
resource "azurerm_management_group_subscription_association" "management" {
  management_group_id = "/providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/contoso-platform-management"
  subscription_id     = "/subscriptions/00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000000"
  depends_on          = [module.alz]
}

Pro Tip: Management Group IDs must be globally unique within your tenant and can only contain letters, digits, and hyphens. Once created, an MG ID is immutable. You can change the Display Name, but changing the ID requires deleting and recreating the group.

4. Deploying with Bicep Deployment Stacks

Bicep users should leverage Deployment Stacks for tenant-scoped deployments. This ensures that when you remove an MG from your code, the stack automatically handles the cleanup in Azure.

targetScope = 'tenant'

module alz 'br/public:avm/ptn/lz/alz:0.1.0' = {
  name: 'alzHierarchyDeploy'
  params: {
    managementGroupName: 'contoso'
    subscriptionPlacement: {
      management: '00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000000'
      # ... other subscriptions ...
    }
  }
}

Deploy the stack via the Azure CLI:

az stack tenant create \
  --name "alz-foundation" \
  --template-file main.bicep \
  --location eastus \
  --action-on-unmanage deleteAll

5. Subscription Placement and Moving State

As workloads evolve, you will inevitably need to move a subscription from Corp to Online or from Sandbox to Production.

Terraform Pattern: Use the moved block to rename resources in your state file without triggering a physical resource recreation. This prevents downtime during architectural refactoring.

moved {
  from = azurerm_management_group_subscription_association.old_path
  to   = azurerm_management_group_subscription_association.new_path
}

Key Takeaways

  1. Job-Based, Not Org-Based: Design your MGs around connectivity and security requirements, not your company’s department list.
  2. Intermediate MG is Mandatory: Always create a container below the Root to avoid locking yourself out of tenant-wide settings.
  3. Audit Before Deny: When applying cascading policies, use Audit mode first to measure impact on existing subscriptions.
  4. Immutability Matters: Choose your MG IDs carefully. You are stuck with them for the life of the landing zone.

Next Steps:

  • Read [Article 2] to build the Hub-and-Spoke network that will connect to these management groups.
  • Read [Article 4] to write the specific Azure Policies that will be assigned to this hierarchy.

Sources

  • Microsoft Learn: ALZ Management Group and Subscription Organization
  • Terraform Registry: avm-ptn-alz Module
  • Azure Service Limits: Management Group Limits
description: Design and deploy a production Azure management group hierarchy with Terraform and Bicep AVM. Covers intermediate groups, platform vs workload separation, and subscription placement.
title: Design Your Azure Management Group and Subscription Hierarchy

content:
Networking is the nervous system of your landing zone. In an enterprise cloud environment, you cannot allow every application to manage its own internet egress or hybrid connectivity. Ad-hoc networking leads to overlapping IP ranges, uninspected traffic, and a fragmented DNS namespace that makes cross-service communication impossible.

The Hub-and-Spoke topology is the mandatory standard for enterprise Azure deployments. It centralizes shared services—such as the Azure Firewall, VPN/ExpressRoute Gateways, and DNS Resolvers—into a single “Hub” VNet. All application workloads reside in “Spoke” VNets, which peer back to the hub to share its connectivity while remaining isolated from one another. This guide teaches you how to build the networking core of your landing zone using Azure Verified Modules (AVM) and establish a secure, scalable perimeter for your cloud workloads.

1. The Hub Networking Stack

The hub VNet is the high-security gateway for your entire Azure region. In 2026, the retirement of “Default Outbound Internet Access” means that a hub with a centralized firewall is no longer just a best practice—it is a requirement for resource connectivity.

The Hub Networking Stack

1.1: Mandatory Reserved Subnets

Azure enforces strict naming and sizing rules for hub services. All of these should be at least a /26 to accommodate future scaling:

  • AzureFirewallSubnet: The data plane for the firewall.
  • AzureFirewallManagementSubnet: Required only if you enable “Forced Tunneling” to route management traffic separately.
  • GatewaySubnet: For VPN or ExpressRoute gateways.
  • AzureBastionSubnet: For the Bastion managed service.

2. Deploying the Hub with Terraform AVM

Using AVM modules reduces the 500 lines of standard VNet/Firewall code into a few atomic blocks.

module "hub_vnet" {
  source  = "Azure/avm-res-network-virtualnetwork/azurerm"
  version = "~> 0.7"

  name                = "vnet-prod-hub-001"
  resource_group_name = "rg-prod-conn-001"
  location            = "eastus"
  address_space       = ["10.0.0.0/16"]

  subnets = {
    AzureFirewallSubnet = { address_prefixes = ["10.0.0.0/26"] }
    GatewaySubnet       = { address_prefixes = ["10.0.0.64/27"] }
    AzureBastionSubnet  = { address_prefixes = ["10.0.0.96/26"] }
  }
}

module "firewall" {
  source  = "Azure/avm-res-network-azurefirewall/azurerm"
  version = "~> 0.5"

  name                = "fw-prod-hub-001"
  resource_group_name = "rg-prod-conn-001"
  location            = "eastus"
  sku_tier            = "Premium" # Required for IDPS and TLS inspection
  
  # Link to the hub subnet
  virtual_network_resource_id = module.hub_vnet.resource_id
}

3. Private DNS Resolution Flow

DNS is the most frequent source of post-deployment support tickets. In a hub-and-spoke model, we centralize Private DNS Zones in the hub and use the Azure DNS Private Resolver to handle queries from both cloud and on-premises clients.

Private DNS Resolution Flow (Resolver Pattern)

By linking your hub VNet to the Private DNS Zones, and configuring your spokes to use the Hub’s Firewall or Resolver as their DNS server, you ensure that resource.privatelink.blob.core.windows.net resolves to a private IP across your entire organization.

4. VNet Peering and Gateway Transit

To connect a spoke to the hub, you must create a two-way peering. In an enterprise landing zone, we enable Gateway Transit. This allows spoke VNets to use the VPN or ExpressRoute gateway sitting in the hub, eliminating the need for expensive gateways in every application subscription.

resource "azurerm_virtual_network_peering" "hub_to_spoke" {
  name                      = "peer-hub-to-app01"
  resource_group_name       = module.hub_vnet.resource_group_name
  virtual_network_name      = module.hub_vnet.name
  remote_virtual_network_id = var.spoke_vnet_id
  allow_gateway_transit     = true
}

5. Controlling Egress with User Defined Routes (UDR)

Merely peering a VNet doesn’t force traffic through the firewall. You must apply a Route Table to your spoke subnets with a default route (0.0.0.0/0) pointing to the Azure Firewall’s private IP. This ensures that every packet leaving the spoke is inspected by your central security policy.

Key Takeaways

  1. Centralize Egress: Use Azure Firewall Premium in the hub to inspect all outbound traffic and enforce FQDN filtering.
  2. Size for Growth: Use /26 for mandatory hub subnets to avoid future downtime during service scaling.
  3. DNS is Global: Use the DNS Private Resolver to provide a unified namespace for cloud and on-premises developers.
  4. Automate Peerings: Include peering and UDR configuration in your Subscription Vending process to ensure new spokes are “Secure by Default.”

Next Steps:

  • Read [Article 3] to design the Identity and RBAC model that will manage these network resources.
  • Read [Article 6] to configure the centralized logging that will capture your Azure Firewall and DNS traffic.

Sources

  • Microsoft Learn: Hub-and-spoke network topology in Azure
  • Azure Verified Modules: Virtual Network Specification
  • Microsoft Learn: Azure DNS Private Resolver Overview
description: Build a production hub-and-spoke network for Azure landing zones. Covers Azure Firewall Premium, Bastion scaling, DNS Private Resolver, and VNet peering with gateway transit.
title: Hub-and-Spoke Networking for Azure Landing Zones: Azure Firewall, Bastion, and Private DNS

content:
Identity is the new perimeter. In a landing zone, network firewalls and VPNs are secondary to the authentication and authorization decisions made at the Entra ID boundary. A single compromised “Owner” account with standing access can bypass every network control you’ve built.

Our identity architecture moves away from permanent, static permissions toward a Zero Standing Access (ZSA) model. We use Privileged Identity Management (PIM) for humans and Workload Identity Federation (OIDC) for machines. This ensures that every high-privilege action is time-bound, auditable, and cryptographic—leaving no static passwords or keys in your environment. This guide teaches you how to design and deploy the identity layer of your landing zone using Azure Verified Modules (AVM) and establish a secure, secret-less foundation for your DevOps teams.

1. Entra ID vs. Azure RBAC

To build a secure model, you must distinguish between the identity provider (Entra ID) and the resource authorization engine (Azure RBAC).

Entra ID vs. Azure RBAC Boundaries

  • Entra ID Roles: Manage the directory (Users, Groups, Domains). Examples: Global Administrator, User Administrator.
  • Azure RBAC Roles: Manage resources inside subscriptions. Examples: Owner, Contributor, Storage Blob Data Reader.

Security Rule: Never use Entra ID admins for daily Azure resource management. Use a dedicated Platform Engineers group in Entra ID and assign it Azure RBAC roles at the Management Group scope.

2. Privileged Identity Management (PIM)

Permanent “Owner” assignments are the #1 risk identified in 2026 security audits. PIM solves this by making users Eligible for a role rather than Active.

PIM JIT Activation Lifecycle

Terraform Implementation: Use the azurerm_pim_eligible_role_assignment resource to automate your platform access.

resource "azurerm_pim_eligible_role_assignment" "platform_admins" {
  scope              = "/providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/contoso-platform"
  role_definition_id = data.azurerm_role_definition.owner.id
  principal_id       = azuread_group.platform_admins.object_id
  
  schedule {
    start_date = "2026-01-01T00:00:00Z"
    expiration {
      type = "AfterDuration"
      duration = "P365D" # Re-evaluate eligibility annually
    }
  }
}

3. Secret-less CI/CD with OIDC

Storing “Client Secrets” in GitHub or Azure DevOps repository settings is a legacy risk. If a secret is leaked, an attacker has permanent access until the secret is rotated. Workload Identity Federation (OIDC) eliminates this entirely.

GitHub OIDC Token Exchange Flow

In 2026, we use User-Assigned Managed Identities for our self-hosted runners and established federated credentials between the runner and your GitHub Environment. This ensures that only a workflow running in your production environment branch on GitHub can acquire the production deployment token.

4. Constraining Administration with RBAC Conditions

The “Owner” role is too powerful. It allows a user to grant other people “Owner” access, effectively bypassing your identity governance. In 2026, we use RBAC Conditions to restrict administrative reach.

Example: Granting “Owner” but blocking the ability to assign the “Owner” or “User Access Administrator” roles to anyone else. This prevents “Privilege Escalation” by your own admins.

resource platformOwner 'Microsoft.Authorization/roleAssignments@2022-04-01' = {
  name: guid(principalId, 'Owner', scope)
  properties: {
    roleDefinitionId: subscriptionResourceId('Microsoft.Authorization/roleDefinitions', '8e3af657-a8ff-443c-a75c-2fe8c4bcb635')
    principalId: principalId
    # Limit what this Owner can assign to others
    conditionVersion: '2.0'
    condition: '((!(ActionMatches{\'Microsoft.Authorization/roleAssignments/write\'})) OR (@Request[Microsoft.Authorization/roleAssignments:RoleDefinitionId] ForAnyOfAnyValues:GuidNotEquals {8e3af657-a8ff-443c-a75c-2fe8c4bcb635, 18d7d88d-d4f5-4b35-97b4-c3f4b4b9b9b6}))'
  }
}

Key Takeaways

  1. Zero Standing Access: No human should have permanent administrative rights in production. Use PIM.
  2. Secret-less Pipelines: Use OIDC for CI/CD. Stop managing AZURE_CLIENT_SECRET in GitHub.
  3. Scoped Identities: Use User-Assigned Managed Identities for platform automation to decouple identity from resource lifecycle.
  4. Conditions are Mandatory: Use RBAC conditions to prevent “Admin sprawl” and ensure your governance remains intact.

Next Steps:

  • Read [Article 4] to use these identities to deploy and enforce Azure Policies.
  • Read [Article 8] to build the GitHub Actions workflow that uses OIDC to deploy your landing zone.

Sources

  • Microsoft Learn: Azure Identity and Access Management Best Practices
  • Microsoft Learn: What is Privileged Identity Management?
  • GitHub Docs: About security hardening with OpenID Connect
description: Design the identity layer of an Azure landing zone. Covers Entra ID vs Azure RBAC, Privileged Identity Management (PIM), and secret-less OIDC authentication for GitHub Actions.
title: Identity and Access Architecture for Azure Landing Zones: Entra ID, RBAC, and PIM

content:
In an enterprise landing zone, configuration drift is the primary enemy. You can build a perfect hub-and-spoke network, but if a developer can manually create a Storage Account with a public endpoint or skip diagnostic logging, your governance model has failed. Azure Policy is the enforcement mechanism that turns your security standards from “recommendations” into “executable logic.”

Azure Policy allows you to govern 1,000 subscriptions with the same effort as one. By assigning policies at the Management Group scope, you ensure that every resource created—today or three years from now—meets your organizational standards. This guide teaches you how to author and deploy policies using Policy as Code patterns in Terraform and Bicep, moving beyond simple auditing to proactive enforcement and automated remediation.

1. The Policy Assignment Hierarchy

Effective governance requires grouping related controls into logical units called Initiatives (or Policy Sets). This allows you to assign a single “Security Baseline” to your hierarchy rather than managing 50 individual assignments.

Policy Assignment Hierarchy

2. Proactive Enforcement with the Deny Effect

The most powerful tool in your governance arsenal is the Deny effect. It evaluates a resource request before it is created. If the resource doesn’t meet the requirement (e.g., it lacks a mandatory tag), ARM rejects the request with a 403 Forbidden.

{
  "if": {
    "field": "Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts/publicNetworkAccess",
    "ne": "Disabled"
  },
  "then": {
    "effect": "Deny"
  }
}

Pro Tip: Always set the mode property in your policy definition. Use Indexed for policies that evaluate tags and locations. Use All for resource-specific properties like network rules or subnets.

3. Self-Healing with DeployIfNotExists (DINE)

Auditing tells you what’s broken; DeployIfNotExists (DINE) fixes it automatically. This effect is used for auxiliary resources like diagnostic settings or private DNS zones. If the primary resource (e.g., a VNet) exists but the dependent resource (e.g., diagnostic settings) is missing, Azure Policy triggers a remediation task to deploy it.

DINE Policy Remediation Workflow

Terraform Implementation: To support DINE, the policy assignment requires a Managed Identity and an RBAC role assignment allowing that identity to deploy resources.

resource "azurerm_management_group_policy_assignment" "logging" {
  name                 = "deploy-diag-settings"
  management_group_id  = var.platform_mg_id
  policy_definition_id = azurerm_policy_definition.dine_logging.id
  location             = "eastus"
  identity {
    type = "SystemAssigned"
  }
}

resource "azurerm_role_assignment" "remediation_access" {
  scope                = var.platform_mg_id
  role_definition_name = "Contributor"
  principal_id         = azurerm_management_group_policy_assignment.logging.identity[0].principal_id
}

4. Policy as Code: Bicep and loadJsonContent()

Maintaining large JSON policy definitions inside Bicep files is difficult. In 2026, we use the loadJsonContent() function to keep our logic in standalone JSON files while managing the deployment in Bicep.

resource secureStoragePolicy 'Microsoft.Authorization/policyDefinitions@2021-06-01' = {
  name: 'deny-public-storage'
  properties: {
    policyRule: loadJsonContent('policies/storage/deny-public-access.json')
    parameters: loadJsonContent('policies/storage/parameters.json')
  }
}

5. Managing Exceptions with Exemptions

Not every application can meet every policy on Day 1. Exemptions allow you to grant temporary waivers for specific resources or subscriptions without loosening the global standard. Unlike “Exclusions” (which are permanent), Exemptions have an expiration date and a categorized reason (Waiver or Mitigated), providing the audit trail required for compliance reviews.

Key Takeaways

  1. Initiatives over Policies: Group your controls to simplify assignment management at scale.
  2. Deny is Prevention: Use Deny for security boundaries (Public IPs, Open RDP) to stop risks before they exist.
  3. DINE is Maintenance: Use DeployIfNotExists for standard platform boilerplate like logging and backup.
  4. Remediate First: Never switch an existing policy to Deny without running a remediation task to fix existing resources first.

Next Steps:

  • Read [Article 5] to see how these policies are automatically applied to new subscriptions during the vending process.
  • Read [Article 9] to learn how to use Azure OpenAI to draft complex custom policy definitions in seconds.

Sources

  • Microsoft Learn: Azure Policy Definition Structure
  • Microsoft Learn: Remediate non-compliant resources
  • Terraform Registry: azurerm_policy_definition
description: Write and deploy Azure Policy definitions, initiatives, and assignments as code. Learn to use the Deny and DeployIfNotExists effects to maintain a secure landing zone foundation.
title: Governance at Scale: Writing and Deploying Azure Policies with Terraform and Bicep

content:
Cloud adoption at scale fails when the platform team is a manual bottleneck. If onboarding a new application team requires a Jira ticket, three emails, and 10 business days of manual configuration, you haven’t built a platform—you’ve built a bureaucracy. In an enterprise landing zone, we treat the Subscription as a product. We use a Subscription Vending pattern to automate the creation and configuration of every new environment through a pull request.

Subscription Vending ensures that every new subscription is “Secure by Default” and “Connected by Design.” When the vending machine runs, it doesn’t just create a billing entry; it places the subscription in the correct Management Group, peers its VNet to the hub, assigns mandatory RBAC roles, and establishes a budget. This guide teaches you how to implement a PR-driven vending workflow using Azure Verified Modules (AVM) and establish a self-service culture for your engineering teams.

1. The PR-Based Vending Workflow

The goal of vending is to move the conversation from “Can you create this for me?” to “Here is the code I want to run.”

PR-Based Vending Workflow

By defining your workloads in a simple YAML or JSON file in a Git repository, you gain a full audit trail of who requested what and when. The platform team merely reviews and approves the PR, letting the automation handle the rest.

2. The Subscription Vending Machine

The vending machine is a modular piece of IaC that coordinates six distinct deployment steps.

The Subscription Vending Machine Architecture

2.1: Creating the Subscription Alias

In 2026, we use the Microsoft.Subscription/aliases resource to provision subscriptions programmatically. This resource is idempotent; if you run it again with the same name, it returns the existing subscription rather than creating a duplicate.

Bicep Pattern:

targetScope = 'tenant'

module subVend 'br/public:avm/ptn/lz/sub-vending:0.5.0' = {
  name: 'subVend-${workloadName}'
  params: {
    subscriptionAliasEnabled: true
    subscriptionBillingScope: '/providers/Microsoft.Billing/billingAccounts/12345/enrollmentAccounts/67890'
    subscriptionDisplayName: workloadName
    subscriptionWorkload: 'Production'
  }
}

3. Automation Patterns: The “90-Second Rule”

A common failure in subscription automation is the Identity Propagation Delay. When you create a new subscription, it takes approximately 60-90 seconds for Entra ID to recognize the new scope. If your IaC immediately attempts to assign a role, it will fail with a PrincipalNotFound or ScopeNotFound error.

Terraform Fix: Use the time_sleep resource to introduce a mandatory pause after subscription creation.

resource "time_sleep" "wait_for_subscription" {
  create_duration = "90s"
  depends_on      = [azurerm_subscription.workload]
}

4. Self-Service IPAM: Avoiding Overlaps

The hardest part of scaling subscription vending is IP Address Management (IPAM). If two teams request the same 10.1.1.0/24 range, your VNet peering will fail.

In 2026, we include a CIDR Overlap Check in our CI/CD pipeline. A Python script reads a central ipam.yaml file in the repo, validates the requested range doesn’t overlap with any existing allocations, and blocks the PR if a collision is detected. This allows teams to choose their own IPs while the platform enforces the global network plan.

5. Security and Billing Guardrails

Vending is the perfect time to apply mandatory controls. Every subscription should receive:

  • RBAC: An “App Admin” group for the workload team.
  • Budget: A $500/month starting budget with automated alerts to the platform team.
  • Diagnostics: A DINE policy (from Article 4) that automatically connects the subscription to the hub’s Log Analytics Workspace.

Key Takeaways

  1. PR-Driven is GitOps: Use Git as the source of truth for your entire cloud footprint.
  2. Idempotency is Safety: Use aliases and AVM pattern modules to ensure your automation can run repeatedly without errors.
  3. Mind the Propagation: Include a 90-second wait after creation to allow the Azure control plane to synchronize.
  4. Enforce CIDR Integrity: Shift IPAM validation into the PR phase to avoid unpeered “Ghost Spokes.”

Next Steps:

  • Read [Article 6] to learn how to monitor the traffic and costs generated by these vended subscriptions.
  • Read [Article 8] to see the complete GitHub Actions pipeline that powers this vending machine.

Sources

  • Microsoft Learn: Subscription vending architecture
  • Bicep Registry: lz/sub-vending module
  • Terraform Registry: azurerm_subscription
description: Automate Azure subscription provisioning with a PR-based vending workflow. Learn to deploy spoke networking, RBAC, and budgets using Terraform and Bicep patterns.
title: Subscription Vending: Automating New Workload Onboarding with IaC

content:
If you can’t see it, you can’t govern it. In an enterprise landing zone, observability is not an afterthought—it is the foundation of security and cost management. A fragmented logging strategy, where every application team manages its own Log Analytics Workspace, leads to “Siloed Observability.” Security teams can’t correlate events across VNets, and finance teams can’t identify the source of a cost spike until the bill arrives.

The Centralized Logging Architecture moves all telemetry—resource logs, activity logs, and network traffic—into a single “Hub” workspace owned by the platform team. This guide teaches you how to design and deploy this hub using Azure Verified Modules (AVM) and establish a self-healing logging baseline using Azure Policy and Data Collection Rules (DCRs).

1. Centralized Logging Architecture

The core design principle is the Hub-and-Spoke Telemetry Flow. While applications run in spokes, their diagnostic “signals” are streamed back to the management subscription.

Centralized Logging Architecture

This centralization enables cross-subscription KQL queries, allowing you to answer questions like “Show me all failed login attempts across the entire production estate” in sub-second time.

2. Deploying the Hub with Terraform AVM

Using AVM ensures your workspace follows best practices for retention and tiering from day one.

module "log_analytics_workspace" {
  source  = "Azure/avm-res-operationalinsights-workspace/azurerm"
  version = "~> 0.4"

  name                = "law-prod-mgt-001"
  resource_group_name = "rg-prod-mgt-001"
  location            = "eastus"
  
  # Standard production settings
  sku               = "PerGB2018"
  retention_in_days = 90
  daily_quota_gb    = 50 # Prevent runaway ingestion costs
}

3. Data Collection Rules (DCR) and Cost Optimization

Logging every packet passing through a firewall is expensive. In 2026, we use Data Collection Rules (DCRs) to filter and transform logs at the ingestion point, before they hit your bill.

DCR Ingestion Filtering (Cost Optimization)

By applying a KQL transform to your firewall logs (e.g., dropping ALLOW logs for internal-to-internal traffic), you can reduce your ingestion costs by 40-60% without losing security visibility into high-risk external traffic.

4. Automating Diagnostics with Azure Policy

You cannot rely on developers to remember to enable diagnostic settings. Instead, use a DeployIfNotExists (DINE) policy (from Article 4) to automatically connect every new VNet, Key Vault, and Storage Account to your central hub.

// Example: Policy existence condition for VNet Diag
{
  "field": "Microsoft.Insights/diagnosticSettings/logs.enabled",
  "equals": "true"
}

5. Visualizing the Platform with Workbooks

Data in Log Analytics is only useful if it’s interpretable. Azure Monitor Workbooks provide an interactive canvas for visualizing your KQL queries. A standard “Platform Health” workbook should include:

  • Top 10 Log Producers: Identify noisy resources that need DCR filtering.
  • RBAC Change Audit: Track every permission change across the management group.
  • Firewall Denials: Map outbound blocks to specific application spokes.

KQL Pro Tip: Use parse_json(tostring(...)) to extract deep metadata from the Properties field in AzureActivity logs, as they are often double-encoded.

Key Takeaways

  1. Hub is the Hub: One Log Analytics Workspace for the entire landing zone is the most efficient design for correlation and cost.
  2. Filter at Ingestion: Use DCRs to drop low-value logs and keep your data clean.
  3. Policy is the Glue: Use DINE policies to ensure 100% logging coverage across new subscriptions.
  4. Workbooks over Dashboards: Use Workbooks for deep, interactive investigation and Dashboards for high-level monitoring.

Next Steps:

  • Read [Article 7] to layer Microsoft Sentinel on top of this data for automated threat detection.
  • Read [Article 9] to implement cost governance queries using this centralized telemetry.

Sources

  • Microsoft Learn: Centralized logging with Azure Monitor
  • Azure Verified Modules: Log Analytics Workspace Specification
  • Microsoft Learn: Data Collection Rules (DCR) overview
description: Design a centralized logging architecture for your Azure Landing Zone using Log Analytics and Data Collection Rules. Learn to automate diagnostic settings at scale.
title: Centralized Monitoring: Log Analytics, Diagnostic Settings, and Azure Monitor Workbooks

content:
A landing zone without a security baseline is a high-speed vehicle without brakes. You can deploy workloads at record pace, but without a centralized system to detect threats and manage posture, your risk surface grows with every new subscription. In an enterprise environment, security cannot be a reactive manual process—it must be a platform-wide service that is “always on.”

The Azure Security Baseline integrates two powerhouse services into a single Unified Security Operations Platform: Microsoft Defender for Cloud (MDC) for Cloud Security Posture Management (CSPM) and Microsoft Sentinel for Security Information and Event Management (SIEM). This guide teaches you how to design and deploy this unified security stack using Azure Verified Modules (AVM) and establish a self-healing security foundation for your entire Azure estate.

1. Unified Security Operations Architecture

The design principle is Defense-in-Depth through Centralization. We use Defender to protect individual workloads and Sentinel to correlate those signals into actionable incidents.

Unified Security Operations Architecture (SIEM + XDR)

2. Cloud Security Posture Management (CSPM)

MDC provides the “Scorecard” for your security. By assigning the Microsoft Cloud Security Benchmark (ASB) at the Management Group scope, you gain real-time visibility into your compliance against hundreds of standard controls (e.g., “MFA should be enabled on accounts with owner permissions”).

Secure Score Tracking Flow

Bicep Implementation: Assigning the baseline at scale requires a Management Group scoped policy assignment with a system-assigned managed identity for DINE remediation.

targetScope = 'managementGroup'

resource defenderInitiativeAssignment 'Microsoft.Authorization/policyAssignments@2023-04-01' = {
  name: 'defender-for-cloud-initiative'
  location: 'eastus'
  identity: {
    type: 'SystemAssigned'
  }
  properties: {
    policyDefinitionId: '/providers/Microsoft.Authorization/policySetDefinitions/1f3afdf9-d0c9-4c3d-847f-89da613e70a8'
  }
}

3. Workload Protection Plans

Beyond posture, you must protect specific resource types. In 2026, we prioritize Agentless Scanning for Servers and SQL. This provides deep visibility into vulnerabilities and malware without the performance overhead or maintenance of a traditional agent.

# Example: Enabling Defender for Servers P2
resource defenderServers 'Microsoft.Security/pricings@2024-01-01' = {
  name: 'VirtualMachines'
  properties: {
    pricingTier: 'Standard'
    subPlan: 'P2' # Enables advanced JIT and EDR features
  }
}

4. Microsoft Sentinel: The Intelligence Layer

Sentinel sits on top of your central Log Analytics Workspace. It uses Analytics Rules to scan your logs for patterns of malicious activity—like a sudden surge in failed logins followed by a successful one from an unusual IP.

Automated Data Connectors

In 2026, the Codeless Connector Framework (CCF) is the standard for data ingestion. Always automate your connectors via Bicep to ensure that every new subscription’s Activity and Defender logs are automatically streamed to the SIEM.

resource activityConnector 'Microsoft.SecurityInsights/dataConnectors@2023-02-01-preview' = {
  name: guid('activity-log-connector')
  kind: 'AzureActivity'
  properties: {
    subscriptionId: subscription().subscriptionId
  }
}

5. Security Posture Tracking with KQL

Use the SecurityResources table in your central hub to track your Secure Score drift over time. This allows you to identify which teams or subscriptions are causing the biggest drops in your security posture and target your remediation efforts.

Key Takeaways

  1. Unified is Simplified: Manage MDC and Sentinel as a single platform to reduce “Console Fatigue.”
  2. Benchmark at Scale: Assign the ASB at the Management Group scope to ensure 100% security coverage.
  3. Go Agentless: Use agentless protection plans to gain security visibility without breaking application performance.
  4. Automate Ingestion: Every new subscription must automatically connect its logs to the SIEM via vended connectors.

Next Steps:

  • Read [Article 8] to implement the CI/CD pipeline that will deploy this security baseline.
  • Read [Article 10] to learn how to remediate the security drift detected by these systems.

Sources

  • Microsoft Learn: What is Microsoft Defender for Cloud?
  • Microsoft Learn: Microsoft Sentinel Overview
  • Azure Verified Modules: Security Baseline Specification
  • Microsoft Cloud Security Benchmark (ASB) v3
description: Establish a robust security baseline by deploying Microsoft Defender for Cloud and Microsoft Sentinel. Learn to automate threat detection and posture management at scale.
title: Security Baseline: Defender for Cloud and Microsoft Sentinel in a Landing Zone

content:
Deploying an enterprise landing zone from a local terminal is a single point of failure. It lacks an audit trail, bypasses peer review, and relies on long-lived administrative secrets stored on personal machines. A production-grade foundation requires a GitOps approach, where every change to the infrastructure is managed as code, tested in a pipeline, and deployed via a secure, secret-less automation identity.

The Landing Zone CI/CD Pipeline transforms your foundation from a collection of scripts into a reliable, repeatable platform service. In 2026, the standard for these pipelines is Workload Identity Federation (OIDC), which eliminates static API keys, and Shift-Left Testing using tools like PSRule. This guide teaches you how to build a production pipeline using GitHub Actions to deploy Azure Verified Modules (AVM) and establish a secure deployment perimeter for your landing zone.

1. The PR-Driven Plan-First Workflow

The goal of the pipeline is to ensure that no change reaches Azure without a human review of the predicted impact. We implement a Plan-First workflow.

PR-Driven Plan-First Workflow

When a developer opens a PR, the pipeline runs a “What-If” analysis and posts the result as a comment. This allows the reviewer to see exactly which resources will be created, modified, or destroyed before clicking merge.

2. Secure Authentication with OIDC

Storing AZURE_CLIENT_SECRET in GitHub is a high-risk legacy pattern. If the secret is leaked, an attacker has permanent access. OpenID Connect (OIDC) solves this by using a trust relationship between GitHub and Entra ID.

GitHub OIDC Security Boundary

In 2026, we use Environment-scoped Federated Credentials. This ensures that the production deployment identity can only be used by a workflow running in the production environment branch on GitHub, providing a cryptographic boundary between your dev and prod infrastructure.

3. Shift-Left Testing with PSRule

Compliance should be caught in the pipeline, not in a post-deployment audit. We use PSRule for Azure to validate our Bicep and Terraform code against the Well-Architected Framework (WAF) before it is deployed.

# GitHub Actions: PSRule WAF Compliance
- name: PSRule WAF Compliance
  uses: microsoft/ps-rule@v2
  with:
    modules: PSRule.Rules.Azure
    inputPath: live/

If a developer attempts to deploy a VNet without a diagnostic setting or an unencrypted Storage Account, PSRule fails the build, preventing the non-compliant code from ever reaching the main branch.

4. Safe State Storage Architecture

Your Terraform state files or Bicep Deployment Stacks contain the blueprint of your entire cloud estate. They must be protected as high-value assets.

Safe State Storage Architecture

By using Private Endpoints and RBAC-only access (disabling storage account keys), you ensure that only your authorized CI/CD runners can read or write the state.

5. Bicep Deployment Stacks: The Native Way

For Bicep users, Deployment Stacks (GA in 2025) provide the state management and “Action on Unmanage” features previously only found in Terraform.

az stack mg create \
  --name "lz-management-layer" \
  --management-group-id "mg-platform" \
  --deny-settings-mode "denyDelete" \
  --action-on-unmanage "deleteResources"

The --deny-settings-mode denyDelete flag acts as a native resource lock, preventing anyone (including owners) from manually deleting resources managed by the stack.

Key Takeaways

  1. Peer Review is Mandatory: Use PR-driven workflows to ensure every platform change has a second pair of eyes.
  2. Go Secret-less: Use OIDC for all automation identities. Treat static client secrets as technical debt.
  3. Validate early: Use PSRule to catch WAF violations in the pipeline rather than the portal.
  4. Protect the State: Isolate your state storage using Private Link and RBAC to prevent tampering.

Next Steps:

  • Read [Article 9] to add cost estimation (Infracost) to your PR comments.
  • Read [Article 10] to implement automated drift detection using these same pipelines.

Sources

  • GitHub Docs: About security hardening with OpenID Connect
  • PSRule for Azure: WAF Compliance Testing
  • Microsoft Learn: Azure Deployment Stacks Overview
  • Azure Verified Modules: CI/CD Standards
description: Build a production-grade CI/CD pipeline for your Azure Landing Zone. Learn to implement OIDC authentication, automated testing with PSRule, and PR-driven workflows.
title: CI/CD Pipeline for Your Landing Zone: Deploying Azure Verified Modules with GitHub Actions

content:
“Unlimited scalability” is a selling point for developers and a liability for finance teams — unless the platform enforces guardrails before spend happens. Most organizations discover this mismatch the hard way: a surprise bill at month-end, a scramble to identify who created what, and a retrospective that produces a spreadsheet nobody maintains.

In an enterprise landing zone, cost governance cannot be reactive. If a resource lacks a required tag, ARM should reject the deployment. If a subscription approaches its budget ceiling, the platform team should know before it crosses, not after. This guide teaches you how to automate cost controls using Azure Verified Modules (AVM), moving beyond simple reporting to proactive enforcement and automated anomaly detection.

1. Tagging Enforcement: The Foundation of Accountability

Every FinOps capability — chargeback, showback, and anomaly detection — depends on tags. A resource without a CostCenter tag cannot be mapped to a business unit.

Why Tags Drive FinOps

A practical enterprise tagging schema uses 3–5 mandatory keys:

  • CostCenter: Maps resource to a billing entity.
  • Environment: Separates cost by lifecycle stage.
  • Owner: Identifies the accountable team or individual.

Azure Cost Management and the FOCUS v1.3 export format all treat these tags as first-class dimensions.

Proactive Tagging with Deny Policies

A Deny policy evaluates the tags property of an ARM request before the resource is created. ARM will return a 403 Forbidden if the mandatory tag is missing.

Pro Tip: Use mode: 'Indexed' in your definition to ensure the policy skips resources that do not support tags (like NICs or managed identities), preventing “Mystery Block” errors during complex deployments.

2. Automating Tag Inheritance

Application teams frequently create child resources—like OS disks or diagnostic settings—that do not inherit tags from their parent Resource Group. A Modify policy closes this gap by automatically copying specified tags from the Resource Group down to every resource created within it.

Tag Inheritance Policy Flow (Modify Effect)

3. Automated Budgeting and Alerts

Every subscription created through the vending process (Article 5) should receive a default budget. A $500/month starting budget with notifications at 50%, 80%, 100%, and 110% provides the platform team with escalating signals before spend becomes a crisis.

Forecasted vs. Actual Alerts

An Actual alert fires when you have already spent the budget. A Forecasted alert fires when the projected month-end cost will exceed the threshold, giving you days to act rather than hours. Configure the 80% Forecasted alert as your primary operational trigger.

Budget Alert Escalation Path

4. Cost Anomaly Detection

In 2026, Azure Cost Management uses deep learning models to distinguish between a legitimate month-end batch spike and an unexpected one (like a developer accidentally deploying a massive VM SKU). Combined with KQL queries against the Usage table, you can identify “Top Movers” and route alerts to your platform SREs for immediate triage.

// KQL: Resource Groups with > $100/day increase
Usage
| where TimeGenerated > ago(7d)
| summarize DailyCost = sum(PreTaxCost) by bin(TimeGenerated, 1d), ResourceGroup
| serialize
| extend PrevDailyCost = prev(DailyCost)
| extend CostChange = DailyCost - PrevDailyCost
| where CostChange > 100
| project Date = TimeGenerated, ResourceGroup, DeltaUSD = round(CostChange, 2)

5. FinOps Visibility with FOCUS v1.3

Standardize your cost exports using the FOCUS v1.3 format. This normalizes Azure cost data into a cloud-agnostic schema, allowing your finance team to use the same reporting tools across multiple clouds without manual field mapping.

Key Takeaways

  1. Tags are the Metadata of Cost: Without enforced tags, FinOps is just guesswork.
  2. Forecast over Actual: Use forecasted alerts to get days of warning instead of hours.
  3. Vending includes Budgets: A subscription without a budget is invisible until the bill arrives.
  4. Audit Mode First: Run your tagging policies in Audit mode for 7 days before switching to Deny.

Next Steps:

  • Read [Article 10] to learn how to maintain these cost controls using automated drift detection.
  • Return to the [Pillar Post] to see how cost governance integrates into the full Landing Zone Blueprint.

Sources

  • FinOps Foundation: FOCUS Specification v1.3
  • Microsoft Learn: Azure Policy Built-in Definitions for Tags
  • Terraform Registry: azurerm_consumption_budget_subscription
  • Bicep Reference: Microsoft.Consumption budgets
description: Master Azure cost governance by automating tagging enforcement, budget alerts, and anomaly detection. Build a FinOps-ready landing zone using Terraform and Bicep.
title: Cost Governance in the Landing Zone: Tagging Enforcement, Budgets, and FinOps Automation

content:
Deploying your landing zone was the easy part. Now you have to live with it.

The most common failure mode in platform engineering is not a bad initial deployment — it is treating the landing zone as a finished project rather than an ongoing product. Azure releases new services and deprecates old ones. Organizational requirements change. Configuration drift is not a hypothetical risk; it is a certainty. Without an operational strategy that anticipates these pressures, a well-governed environment gradually becomes a collection of special cases and undocumented manual changes. This guide teaches you the habits and technical patterns required to maintain a healthy, evolving foundation.

1. Managing Configuration Drift

Drift takes two forms: IaC State Drift (code vs. actual resources) and Policy Compliance Drift (actual resources vs. security standards).

Automated Drift Remediation Loop

Run drift scans weekly using terraform plan --refresh-only or Bicep what-if. A scheduled GitHub Actions workflow that opens a GitHub Issue when drift is found provides the audit trail and visibility needed for incident response.

2. Remediating Drift Without Downtime

When drift is detected, you must decide whether to import the manual change into your code or revert the resource to its authorized state.

  • Accepting the change: Use Terraform’s import block to bring a portal-made change into your state file.
  • Rejecting the change: Update your YAML/Bicep logic to the desired state and re-deploy.
  • Policy remediation: Use Remediation Tasks to fix auxiliary resource gaps (like missing logs) without touching the primary resource.
# Example: Bulk remediation of diagnostic settings
az policy remediation create \
  --name "fix-logging-baseline-$(date +%Y%m%d)" \
  --policy-assignment "/providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/mg-intermediate/providers/Microsoft.Authorization/policyAssignments/deploy-vnet-diagnostic-settings" \
  --resource-discovery-mode ReEvaluateCompliance \
  --resource-type "Microsoft.Network/virtualNetworks"

3. The Migration Path: Moving to Azure Verified Modules (AVM)

In 2026, the legacy CAF modules are archived. Migration to AVM is a maintenance requirement. A naive migration destroys and recreates resources, but the Terraform moved block allows you to remap the state address without any physical resource impact.

AVM Module Migration (The moved Block)

Bicep Pattern: Use Deployment Stacks to manage the transition. The stack reconciles the template change and updates existing resources in-place rather than replacing them.

4. Governance and Identity Lifecycle

The average landing zone accumulates RBAC cruft faster than any other category. Every quarter, your platform team should perform two key reviews:

  1. Orphaned Role Review: Use PowerShell to find role assignments linked to “Identity not found” (deleted) principals and remove them.
  2. Access Review: Use Entra ID Access Reviews (P2) to force on-call engineers to justify their PIM eligibility every 90 days. A “Deny-by-Inaction” default is the safest posture.

5. Hub Evolution: Scaling to Multi-Region

When you expand to a second Azure region, the decision between Global VNet Peering (for simplicity) and Azure Virtual WAN (for massive scale) is your primary architectural pivot. For most organizations, regional hub-to-hub peering is sufficient for up to 4 regions.

# Adding a secondary hub is additive logic
module "hub_secondary" {
  source = "./modules/hub-networking"
  location = "westeurope"
  address_space = ["10.1.0.0/16"]
  peer_to_hub_id = module.hub_primary.vnet_id
}

Key Takeaways

  1. Platform as a Product: Give your landing zone a roadmap and a dedicated maintenance window.
  2. Weekly Drift Scans: Catch deviations in days, not months.
  3. Remediate via Policy: Avoid manual “Quick Fixes.” Use the policy engine to maintain the audit trail.
  4. Pin Your Versions: Never use latest for AVM modules. Use Renovate or Dependabot to manage upgrades as code.

The Azure Platform Engineering series is complete. You have built a production-ready foundation from scratch, secured it with Zero Trust identity, and established the operational habits to keep it healthy as you scale.

Sources

  • Microsoft Learn: Cloud Adoption Framework - Governance and Operations
  • Terraform Documentation: Refactoring with moved blocks
  • Azure Verified Modules: Registry
  • Microsoft Learn: Azure Policy Remediation
description: Learn how to operate, maintain, and upgrade your Azure Landing Zone. Covers policy drift remediation, RBAC reviews, and migrating to Azure Verified Modules (AVM).
title: Day-2 Operations: Maintaining and Evolving Your Azure Landing Zone

content:
I am a Senior DevOps Engineer and Azure Solutions Architect specializing in Platform Engineering and PowerShell automation. My mission is to help organizations build governed, scalable, and production-ready Azure environments using modern Infrastructure as Code (IaC) patterns like Terraform and Bicep.

With over a decade of experience in the Microsoft ecosystem, I focus on the “Day 0” to “Day 2” lifecycle of cloud infrastructure, ensuring that security, governance, and cost-optimization are baked into every deployment.

Core Technical Expertise:

  • Azure Platform Engineering (CAF/ALZ)
  • PowerShell & Automation
  • Terraform & Bicep (AVM)
  • DevOps CI/CD (GitHub Actions/ADOs)
  • Cloud Governance & Policy
description: Senior DevOps Engineer and Azure Solutions Architect specializing in Platform Engineering and PowerShell automation.
title: About Larry James Henry
